content:
While I'm reading the code in rust/kernel/file.rs, I find this struct "pub struct File { pub(crate) ptr: *mut bindings::file,}".
I wonder why using a pointer to struct rather than a struct directly? Ex "pub struct File { pub(crate) file: bindings::file,}".
Is it related to saving the memory space?
Thanks for your help in advance!
That file struct is "owned" by other code in Linux kernel
Notice there is only one way to construct a File by using from_fd

Thanks for your help sincerely! After some thinking and investigation, I convince myself finally. But I don't know whether the reason is right.

It seems most of the functions related to the struct file in the Linux kernel use a pointer to the struct file as the argument or return value. The reason for this is to decrease the memory size of the parameters. Therefore, in the bindings_generated.rs, the function parameter related to the struct file is "*mut file". Therefore, in the file.rs we use the struct File to wrap the pointer, which helps us from writing duplicated codes.
We don't need to write *mut bindings::file every time. Instead, we just need to write "self.ptr" to represent *mut bindings::file in the impl File block.

Well, I would say memory usage concern is secondary in Linux kernel (not specific to Rust code). It doesn't make sense to have multiple copies of the same thing. There should be one canonical copy that holds all the information.

Rust for Linux consumers (as in, engineers not working on core abstractions) shouldn't really try to mess with raw pointers. The File abstraction is more about providing safe abstraction for consumers. The reason is not about avoiding code duplication.
I get your point and it is more reasonable. I will accept it. Thanks again for your time!
Hongyu Li has marked this topic as resolved.

Wei Liu said:

That file struct is "owned" by other code in Linux kernel

Could you elaborate this further? Is this a result of the bindgen?
No. Nothing to do with bindgen or Rust. Like I said, there is only one copy (instance) of a certain file struct. Its life cycle is carefully managed.

I am sorry but I don't fully understand your explanation. For example, where is the difference between clk.rs and device.rs.
I see the different kinds of constructors in rust but I don't see the difference from the C sources clk.c and device.h
I don't understand what you're confused about, sorry.

I am confused about the fact that the struct is owned by the kernel and that I have to use a pointer to it, that's not how it works in the C world.
Also I don't understand where the difference in the above examples lays, clearly the device constructor of device.rs is restricted to the result of the get function, clock.rs is not. I don't see where the difference come from.
How do you expect it to work in the C world?
I instantiate a new object from the definition provided in the header file
And then, what would you do if you only want to keep a single instance of it that holds the truths?
Of course I would then crate a pointer to it. I think that is the point I am confused of. Why should there be a single instance that holds the truth?
Imaging the structure represent a file. How do you know how large a file is when there are two copies of struct file that say they hold the truths?
One copy says the file is 100 bytes and the other says 101 bytes. Which one would you believe?

You cannot distinguish them. But what has this to do with the template of the File? You are talking about the instanttaions of files as objects, there this makes sense to me. But when I want to create a new file object, why should I point to another object, I want to create a new one?
I don't know how to explain further I'm afraid.
Last I checked, there is no method in Rust in Linux to create a file.
It only has a from_fd method.
I think I can grasp what you mean. I will read in this further. Thank you for your time and explanation!
No problem. Good luck!
